کبریت.e: From Classical Persian کبریت (kibrīt), from Arabic كِبْرِيت (kibrīt).

senses.g: Kabal (a city in Swat district, province of Khyber Pakhtunkhwa, Pakistan)

کبوتر.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian کَبُوتَر (kabūtar, “pigeon; dove”).
Borrowed from Classical Persian کَبُوتَر (kabūtar, “pigeon; dove”).

کبیر.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian کبیر (kabīr), from Arabic كَبِير (kabīr).

senses.g: One of the 99 names of Allah
Kabir, an Indian poet and religious leader

کبیر.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian کبیر (kabīr), from Arabic كَبِير (kabīr).

ex.u:
آنکھوں میں مروت تری اے یار کہاں ہے
پوچھا نہ کبھو مجھ کو وہ بیمار کہاں ہے
ā̃khõ mẽ muravvat tirī ai yār kahā̃ hai
ex.r: pūchā na kabhū mujh ko voh bīmār kahā̃ hai
ex.e: (please add an English translation of this quotation)

کبھو.e: Borrowed from Braj कभू (kabhū), a compound of कब (kab) + हू (hū).

کپاس.e: Inherited from Sauraseni Prakrit 𑀓𑀧𑁆𑀧𑀸𑀲 (kappāsa), from Sanskrit कर्पास (karpāsa, “cotton”), ultimately from Munda. Possibly related to Sanskrit कर्पट (karpaṭa), whence Urdu کَپْڑَا (kapṛā, “cloth, garment”).

کپڑا.e: Inherited from Sauraseni Prakrit 𑀓𑀧𑁆𑀧𑀟 (kappaḍa) + Middle Indo-Aryan -𑀓- (-ka-), from Sanskrit कर्पट (karpaṭa, “garment, rag”). Cognate with Marathi कपडा (kapḍā), कापड (kāpaḍ).

ex.u: میرَا کُتَّا ہَمیشَہ بِلِّی پَر بَھوںکْتَا ہَےْ۔
ex.r: merā kuttā hameśa billī par bha͠uktā hai.
ex.e: My dog always barks at a cat.

کتاب.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian کِتَاب (kitāb), borrowed from Arabic كِتَاب (kitāb). Displaced Old Hindi कतेब (kateba), borrowed from the same term. Compare Punjabi کتاب (kitāb), Sindhi ڪِتابُ (kitābu).

کتاب مقدس.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian کِتابِ مُقَدَّس (kitāb-i muqaddas), from Arabic الْكِتَابُ الْمُقَدَّس (al-kitābu l-muqaddas, literally “the holy scripture”).

کتب خانہ.e: From Classical Persian کتبخانه (kutub-xāna), a variant of کتابخانه (kitāb-xāna) using the Arabic plural کتب (kutub).

کتنا.e: Inherited from Prakrit 𑀓𑀺𑀢𑁆𑀢𑀺𑀬 (kittiya) + Middle Indo-Aryan -𑀦- (-na-), from Sanskrit *कियत्त (*kiyatta), variant of कियत् (kíyat). Doublet of کِتَّا (kittā). Cognate with Punjabi ਕਿਤਨਾ /کِتْنا (kitnā).

کتی.e: Learned borrowing from Sanskrit कति (kati), ultimately from Proto-Indo-European *kʷóti.

کٹھل.e: Inherited from Sanskrit कण्टफल (kaṇṭaphala, literally “thorny fruit”); cognate with Assamese কঁঠাল (kõthal), Bengali কাঁঠাল (kãṭhal), Bhojpuri कटहर (kaṭᵊhar), and Odia କଠଲ (kaṭhala).

کثرت.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian کثرت (kasrat), from Arabic كَثْرَة (kaṯra).

کجرا.e: Inherited from Prakrit 𑀓𑀚𑁆𑀚𑀮 (kajjala) + Middle Indo-Aryan -𑀓- (-ka-), from Sanskrit कज्जल (kajjala).

کچا.e: Inherited from Ashokan Prakrit *𑀓𑀘𑁆𑀘- (*kacca-). Compare Sanskrit काषण (kāṣaṇa, “unripe”).

کچھ.e: From, undergoing metathesis, Old Hindi कछु (kachu), from Sauraseni Prakrit 𑀓𑀺𑀁𑀘𑀺 (kiṃci), from Sanskrit किंचिद् (kiṃcid), neuter singular of कश्चिद् (kaścid, “someone”) (whence کوئی (koī, “anyone”)). The -ch- reflects -śc- in Sanskrit कश्चिद् (kaścid).

From, undergoing metathesis, Old Hindi कछु (kachu), from Sauraseni Prakrit 𑀓𑀺𑀁𑀘𑀺 (kiṃci), from Sanskrit किंचिद् (kiṃcid), neuter singular of कश्चिद् (kaścid, “someone”) (whence کوئی (koī, “anyone”)). The -ch- reflects -śc- in Sanskrit कश्चिद् (kaścid).

From, undergoing metathesis, Old Hindi कछु (kachu), from Sauraseni Prakrit 𑀓𑀺𑀁𑀘𑀺 (kiṃci), from Sanskrit किंचिद् (kiṃcid), neuter singular of कश्चिद् (kaścid, “someone”) (whence کوئی (koī, “anyone”)). The -ch- reflects -śc- in Sanskrit कश्चिद् (kaścid).

کچھوا.e: Inherited from Sauraseni Prakrit 𑀓𑀘𑁆𑀙𑀯 (kacchava), from Sanskrit कच्छप (kacchapa), from Proto-Indo-Iranian *kaćyápas. Doublet of کشف (kaśaf).

senses.g: inflection of کرنا (karnā, “to do”): — second-person singular present imperative

کراٹے.e: Borrowed from English karate, from Japanese 空手, from 唐手 (karate), from Okinawan 唐手 (tūdī).

senses.g: Karachi (the largest city in Pakistan situated along the Arabian Sea, as well as the provincial capital of Sindh Province)

کراچی.e: Borrowed from Sindhi ڪراچي (karācī) / कराची, from earlier ڪولاچي (kolācī) / कोलाची, named after Mai Kolachi.

کراہنا.e: Unknown. Possibly connected with Sanskrit कारयति (kārayati, “calls”), itself from कृ (kṛ, “to praise”).

کرایہ دار.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian کِرَایَه دَار (kirāya dār).

senses.g: a skilled worker who makes things by hand

کردار.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian کِرْدَار (kirdār). First attested in c. 1649 as Middle Hindi کردار (krdar).

senses.g: Kurdistan (a cultural region in West Asia inhabited mostly by the Kurds, encompassing parts of Turkey, Iraq, Iran and Syria)
Kurdistan (an autonomous region in northern and northeastern Iraq, lying within the aforementioned Kurdish-majority region)
Kurdistan (a province in northwestern Iran, lying within the aforementioned Kurdish-majority region)

کردستان.e: From کرد (Kurd) + ـستان (-istān, “land of...”), or possibly borrowed from Classical Persian کردستان (kurdistān).

senses.g: a word ending in a kŕt affix (i.e. an affix added to verbal roots to form substantives or adjectives)

کرسی.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian کُرْسِی (kursī), borrowed from Arabic كُرْسِيّ (kursiyy), borrowed from Aramaic כּוּרְסְיָא (kursəyā) or Classical Syriac ܟܘܪܣܝܐ (kursəyā).

کرشمہ.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian کرشمه (kirišma), probably from Ancient Greek χᾰ́ρῐσμᾰ (khắrĭsmă, “grace, favour, gift”), from χᾰρῐ́ζομαι (khărĭ́zomai, “I show favor”), from χᾰ́ρῐς (khắrĭs, “grace”), from χαίρω (khaírō, “I am happy”).

senses.g: a female given name, Karishma, from Persian

کرشمہ.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian کرشمه (kirišma), probably from Ancient Greek χᾰ́ρῐσμᾰ (khắrĭsmă, “grace, favour, gift”), from χᾰρῐ́ζομαι (khărĭ́zomai, “I show favor”), from χᾰ́ρῐς (khắrĭs, “grace”), from χαίρω (khaírō, “I am happy”).

senses.g: a male given name, Krishna, from Sanskrit, of Hindu usage

کرشن.e: Learned borrowing from Sanskrit कृष्ण (kṛṣṇa). Doublet of کنھیا (kanhaiyā, inherited) and کشن (kiśan, semi-learned borrowing).

senses.g: Kyrgyzstan (a country in Central Asia; capital: Bishkek)

کرم.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian کَرَم (karam), from Arabic كَرَم.

senses.g: ray, beam (of light, of the sun or moon etc.)

کرن.e: Borrowed from Sanskrit किरण (kiraṇa). First attested in c. 1518 as Middle Hindi کرن (krn /⁠kiran⁠/).
Semi-learned borrowing from Sanskrit कर्ण (karṇa). First attested in c. 1657 as Middle Hindi کرن (krn /⁠karan⁠/).
Semi-learned borrowing from Sanskrit करण (karaṇa). First attested in c. 1611 as Middle Hindi کرن (krn /⁠karan⁠/).

ex.u: تُم کْیا کَرْتے ہو؟
ex.e: What do you do?

کرنا.e: Inherited from Prakrit 𑀓𑀭𑁂𑀇 (karei), from Ashokan Prakrit 𑀓𑀭𑁄𑀢𑀺 (karoti), from Sanskrit क॒रोति॑ (karóti), from कृ॒णोति॑ (kṛṇóti), from Proto-Indo-Iranian *kr̥náwti, from Proto-Indo-European *kʷer- + *-néwti. Cognate with Punjabi ਕਰਨਾ (karnā), Nepali गर्नु (garnu), Persian کردن (kardan).

senses.g: Kuru, a legendary king in the Mahabharata who is the progenitor of the Kauravas
a city in Haryana, India, the domain of the Kaurava clan and kingdom; famous for the mythical War of Kurukshetra.

کرو کشیتر.e: Learned borrowing from Sanskrit कुरुक्षेत्र (kurukṣetra, literally “realm of Kuru”).

senses.g: to cause to do, to make do or cause, to have or get done or made

ex.u: میری اُس سے مُلاقات کَرواؤ گی کْیا؟
ex.r: merī us se mulāqāt karvāo gī kyā?
ex.e: Will you introduce me to her?

کروانا.e: Causative form of کرنا (karnā) with stem augmented by وا (vā)

کروڑ.e: Inherited from Sauraseni Prakrit 𑀓𑁄𑀟𑀺 (koḍi), from Sanskrit कोटि (koṭi), of Munda origin. Compare Gujarati કરોડ (karoḍ), Marathi करोड (karoḍ) and Nepali करोड (karoḍ).
Inherited from Sauraseni Prakrit 𑀓𑁄𑀟𑀺 (koḍi), from Sanskrit कोटि (koṭi), of Munda origin. Compare Gujarati કરોડ (karoḍ), Marathi करोड (karoḍ) and Nepali करोड (karoḍ).

senses.g: Croatia (a country on the Balkan Peninsula in Southeast Europe)

کری.e: Borrowed from the nominative singular of Sanskrit करिन् (karin), from कर (kara) + -इन् (-in). कर (kara) is from the root कृ (kṛ), while the suffix -इन् (-in) is from Proto-Indo-European *-iHnos. Related to کَر (kar, “hand, elephant's trunk”).

senses.g: a religious act or ceremony, a pious act, a duty
a male given name, Karim or Kareem, from Arabic

کڑوا.e: Inherited from Middle Hindi کڙوا (kṛva), from Old Hindi करवा (karavā), from Prakrit 𑀓𑀟𑀼𑀅 (kaḍua), from Sanskrit कटुक (kaṭuka).

کڑوا پن.e: Inherited from Old Hindi करवापण (karavāpaṇa). By surface analysis, کَڑْوَا (kaṛvā) + ـپَن (-pan).

کس.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian کُس (kus, “pussy, cunt”).

ex.u: کسان اپنی فصل منڈی میں لاتے ہیں
ex.e: Farmers bring their yield to the market.

کسان.e: Inherited from Sauraseni Prakrit, from Sanskrit कृषाण (kṛṣāṇa).

senses.g: oblique of کوئی (koī): some, a certain, a given

کشتی.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian کشتی (kaštī, kištī).
Borrowed from Classical Persian کشتی (kuštī). Cognate with Marathi कुस्ती (kustī).

کشف.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian کَشْف (kašf), borrowed from Arabic كَشْف (kašf). First attested in c. 1564 as Middle Hindi کشف (kśf /⁠kaśf⁠/).

senses.g: a female given name, Kashaf, from Arabic

کشف.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian کَشْف (kašf), borrowed from Arabic كَشْف (kašf). First attested in c. 1564 as Middle Hindi کشف (kśf /⁠kaśf⁠/).
Borrowed from Classical Persian کَشَف (kašaf). Doublet of کَچْھوَا (kachvā)

senses.g: Kashmir (a contested geographic region of South Asia in the northern part of the Indian subcontinent, located between (and de facto divided between) India, Pakistan and China) — Azad Kashmir (an autonomous territory administered by Pakistan; capital: Muzaffarabad)

Kashmir (a contested geographic region of South Asia in the northern part of the Indian subcontinent, located between (and de facto divided between) India, Pakistan and China) — Jammu Kashmir (a union territory of India; capitals: Srinagar and Jammu)

کشمیر.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian کشمیر (kašmīr), potentially also a learned borrowing from Sanskrit कश्मीर (kaśmīra), which is probably a contraction of कश्यपमीर (kaśyapamīra) or कश्यपमेरु (kaśyapameru). Compare Kashmiri کٔشیٖر (kạśīr).

senses.g: a male given name, Kishan, from Sanskrit, of Hindu usage

کشن.e: Semi-learned borrowing from Sanskrit कृष्ण (kṛṣṇa). Doublet of کرشن (kirśn) and کنھیا (kanhaiyā).

کشور.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian کِشْوَر (kišwar, “realm, region; country; kingdom, territory; clime”), from Middle Persian 𐭪𐭱𐭥𐭥 (kišwar), from Avestan 𐬐𐬀𐬭𐬀𐬱𐬀𐬎𐬎𐬀𐬭𐬀 (karšvar, “realm, region; one of the seven realms of the world”), from the root 𐬐𐬀𐬭𐬀𐬱𐬀 (karš-, “to plough”) Cognate Pashto کِشْوَر (kišwár), Central Kurdish کیشوهر (kîşwer, “continent”), and Sogdian [script needed] (kyšp /⁠kišp⁠/, “country”).

کشیدگی.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian کشیدگی (kašīdagī), equivalent to کشید (kaśīd) + ـگی (-gī).

کعبہ.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian کعبه (ka'ba), from Arabic كَعْبَة (kaʕba). First attested as Old Hindi کعبہ (k'bh). Compare Punjabi ਕਾਅਬਾ (kāabā) / کَعبَہ (kaʻbah), Sindhi ڪَعبو (kaʻbo), Gujarati કાબા (kābā), Bengali কাবা (kaba).

senses.g: penance; atonement; expiation (ie. for committing a sin)

کفارہ.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian کَفَّارَه (kaffāra), from Arabic كَفَّارَة (kaffāra). Doublet of کَفَّارَت (kaffārat).

senses.g: tomorrow — in the future; later on

کل.e: From Classical Persian کل (kull), from Arabic كُلّ (kull).
From Classical Persian کل (kull), from Arabic كُلّ (kull).

کلاشنکوف.e: Corrupted from Russian Калашников (Kalašnikov), possibly through or influenced by English Kalashnikov.

senses.g: A rope tied around an elephant's neck to control it.

کلاوا.e: Inherited from Prakrit 𑀓𑀮𑀸𑀯 (kalāva), from Sanskrit कलाप (kalāpa). First attested in c. 1603 as Middle Hindi کلاوا (klava). Cognate with Marathi कळवा (kaḷvā), Punjabi کلاوا (kalāvā).

کلائی.e: Inherited from Sauraseni Prakrit 𑀓𑀮𑀸𑀇𑀆 (kalāiā), from Sanskrit कलाची (kalācī, “forearm”).

کلبھ.e: Inherited from Sauraseni Prakrit 𑀓𑀮𑀪 (kalabha), from Sanskrit कलभ (kalabha).

senses.g: Kolkata (a megacity, the capital of West Bengal, India)

کلکتہ.e: Borrowed from English Calcutta, ultimately from Bengali কলকাতা (kolkata). Doublet of کولکاتا (kolkātā)

کلمہ.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian کَلِمَه (kalima), from Arabic كَلِمَة (kalima).

senses.g: black, dark, someone of dark skin

کلی.e: Inherited from Sauraseni Prakrit 𑀓𑀮𑀺𑀕 (kaliga), from Sanskrit कलिका (kalikā). Cognate with Gujarati કળી (kaḷī), Marathi कळी (kaḷī).
Feminine form of کُلّا (kullā), from Sanskrit कवल (kavala).

senses.g: kalyan (a thaat in classical Hindustani music)

ex.u: اس کے پاس پتھر کا کلیجا ہے۔
ex.r: uske pās patthar kā kalejā hai
ex.e: He has a heart of stone.

کلیجا.e: From Sanskrit कालेय (kāleya). Compare Bhojpuri 𑂍𑂩𑂵𑂔𑂰 (karejā), Magahi 𑂍𑂩𑂵𑂔 (karej), Assamese কলিজা (koliza), Marathi काळीज (kāḷīj), Bengali কলিজা (kolija), Punjabi کالجا (kāljā).

کلیدی تختہ.e: Calque of English keyboard; compound of کلیدی (“key”) + تختہ (“board”).

senses.g: the Church; the combined Christian community.

کلیسیا.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian کَلِیسَا (kalīsā), borrowed from Ancient Greek ἐκκλησία (ekklēsía), from ἔκκλητος (ékklētos) + -ῐ́ᾱ (-ĭ́ā). Doublet of گرجا (girjā).

senses.g: well, sound, healthy, free from sickness; recovered from sickness, convalescent

کلیہ.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian کلیه (kulliyya), from Arabic كُلِّيَّة (kulliyya).
Borrowed from Classical Persian کلیه (kulliyya), from Arabic كُلِّيَّة (kulliyya).

senses.g: less (in lower degree; to smaller extent)

کم.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian کم (kam, “little, few”).

senses.g: less (in lower degree; to smaller extent)

کم.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian کم (kam, “little, few”).
Borrowed from Classical Persian کم (kam, “little, few”).

کم گو.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian کَم گو (kam gō).

کمال.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian کمال (kamāl), from Arabic verbal noun كَمَال (kamāl).
Borrowed from Classical Persian کمال (kamāl), from Arabic verbal noun كَمَال (kamāl).

senses.g: Kamalia (a city in Toba Tek Singh district, Punjab, Pakistan)

ex.u: کِدھَر سے چُونَا یِہ کَمْبَخْت جُمْلَہ؟
ex.r: kidhar se cūnā ye kambaxt jumla?
ex.e: From where did you pick up this wretched sentence?

کمبخت.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian کمبخت (kam-baxt). By surface analysis, کم + بخت. Compare Punjabi ਕੰਬਖ਼ਤ (kambxat) / کَمبَخت (kambaxt), Gujarati કમબખ્ત (kambakht).

ex.u: کَون کَمْبَخْت کُرْتے کے سَاتھ دھوتِی پَہَنتا ہَے؟
ex.r: kaun kambaxt kurte ke sāth dhotī pahantā hai?
ex.e: Who the hell wears a dhoti with a kurta?

کمبخت.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian کمبخت (kam-baxt). By surface analysis, کم + بخت. Compare Punjabi ਕੰਬਖ਼ਤ (kambxat) / کَمبَخت (kambaxt), Gujarati કમબખ્ત (kambakht).

senses.g: comparative degree of کَم (kam): less (than), lesser

کمر.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian کَمَر (kamar). First attested in c. 1635 as Middle Hindi کمر (kmr /⁠kamar⁠/). Displaced Middle Hindi لنک (lãk, “waist”).

ex.u: تمہاری چیزیں میرے کمرے میں ہیں۔
ex.r: tumhārī cīzẽ mere kamre mẽ ha͠i.
ex.e: Your things are in my room.

کمرہ.e: Borrowed from Portuguese câmara, from Old Galician-Portuguese camara, from Latin camera.

senses.g: Wales (a country in the United Kingdom)

کمزور.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian کمزور (kamzor). By surface analysis, کم (kam, “little, less”) + زور (zor, “strength, power”).

کمزوری.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian کَمْزورِی (kamzōrī), from کَمْزور (kamzōr) + ـِی (-ī).

senses.g: Comilla (a city in Comilla district, Bangladesh)
One of the eleven districts in the Chittagong Division of Bangladesh.
derogatory, meaning belonging to the lowest caste.

کمیٹی.e: Corruption of English committee (possibly through Punjabi کَمیٹی (kameṭī)).

کمھڑا.e: Inherited from Sauraseni Prakrit 𑀓𑀼𑀫𑁆𑀳𑀁𑀟 (kumhaṃḍa), ultimately from a substrate language. The Middle Indo-Aryan word was hypercorrected into कुष्माण्ड (kuṣmāṇḍa). Compare Bengali কুমড়া (kumṛa).

senses.g: to wither, to wilt, to fade away

ex.u: پُھول کُمْھلانا
ex.e: flower wilting

senses.g: to cause to wither, to cause to wilt, to cause to fade away
(of the heart or face) to sink, droop

کمھلانا.e: From Sauraseni Prakrit *𑀓𑁄𑀫𑁆𑀳𑁆𑀮 (*komhla) + ـآ (-ā, causative augment) + ـنا (-nā, verbal suffix), the first element of which is probably from Sanskrit कश्मल (kaśmala).

senses.g: "Be! And thus, it is." (God states in the Qur'an) – used to state God's power over its creation.

ex.u:
سَچ تُو ہی ہَے، خُدایَا
کُن فَیَکُون
ex.r:
sac tū hī hai, xudāyā
kun fayakūn
ex.e:
You are The Truth, O God
Be, and it is!

کن فیکون.e: Unadapted borrowing from Arabic كُنْ فَيَكُونُ (kun fayakūnu). First attested in c. 1503 as Middle Hindi کن فیکون (kn fykvn).

کنٹرول.e: Borrowed from English control, possibly influenced by other languages.

senses.g: a cave, cavern, abyss, chasm (in a mountain)
Kandhkot (a city in Kashmore district, Sindh, Pakistan)
A river in Pakistan and Afghanistan
synagogue; fire temple; church; temple/mandir; gurdwara

کنشت.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian کنشت (kuništ), from Aramaic כְּנִישְׁתָּא (kənīštā).

کنفیوشس.e: Borrowed from Latin Confucius, from Mandarin 孔夫子 (Kǒng Fūzǐ, “Master Kong”).

کنگال.e: Borrowed from Northwestern Indo-Aryan (whence Punjabi کنگال (kaṅgāl)), from Prakrit 𑀓𑀁𑀓𑀸𑀮 (kaṃkāla), from Sanskrit कङ्काल (kaṅkāla, “skeleton”). Cognate with Gujarati કંગાલ (kaṅgāl), Bengali কাঙাল (kaṅal), Assamese কঙাল (koṅal), and possibly Marathi कंकाळ (kaṅkāḷ, “cruel”).

Borrowed from Northwestern Indo-Aryan (whence Punjabi کنگال (kaṅgāl)), from Prakrit 𑀓𑀁𑀓𑀸𑀮 (kaṃkāla), from Sanskrit कङ्काल (kaṅkāla, “skeleton”). Cognate with Gujarati કંગાલ (kaṅgāl), Bengali কাঙাল (kaṅal), Assamese কঙাল (koṅal), and possibly Marathi कंकाळ (kaṅkāḷ, “cruel”).

senses.g: bangle, bracelet, usually made of metal

کنگن.e: Borrowed from Punjabi کنگن (kngn), from Prakrit 𑀓𑀁𑀓𑀡 (kaṃkaṇa), from Sanskrit कङ्कण (kaṅkaṇa).

ex.u: کنواری مریم
ex.e: Virgin Mary

senses.g: well (hole in the ground for fetching water)

کنواں.e: Inherited from Old Hindi कुआ (kuā), from Sauraseni Prakrit 𑀓𑀽𑀯𑀅 (kūvaa), 𑀓𑀽𑀯𑀕 (kūvaga), from Sanskrit कूपक (kūpaka).

کنور.e: Inherited from Sauraseni Prakrit 𑀓𑀼𑀫𑀸𑀭 (kumāra), from Sanskrit कुमार (kumārá). Doublet of کمار (kumār).

کنول.e: Inherited from Old Hindi कवल (kavala), from Apabhramsa कमल (kamala), from Prakrit 𑀓𑀫𑀮 (kamala), from Sanskrit कमल (kamala). Doublet of کَمَل (kamal).

ex.u: کَنْہَیَّا کا بالْپَن
ex.e: infancy of Krishna

senses.g: a male given name, Kanhaiya, from Sanskrit, of Hindu usage

کنہیا.e: Diminutive of कांह (kāṃha)/कान्ह (kānha), from Prakrit 𑀓𑀡𑁆𑀳 (kaṇha), from Sanskrit कृष्ण (kṛṣṇa). Doublet of کرشن (kirśn, learned borrowing) and کشن (kiśan, semi-learned borrowing). Cognate to Assamese কানু (kanu), কানাই (kanai), Bengali কান (kano), কানাই (kanai), Gujarati કાનો (kāno), કાનુડો (kānuḍo).

Diminutive of कांह (kāṃha)/कान्ह (kānha), from Prakrit 𑀓𑀡𑁆𑀳 (kaṇha), from Sanskrit कृष्ण (kṛṣṇa). Doublet of کرشن (kirśn, learned borrowing) and کشن (kiśan, semi-learned borrowing). Cognate to Assamese কানু (kanu), কানাই (kanai), Bengali কান (kano), কানাই (kanai), Gujarati કાનો (kāno), કાનુડો (kānuḍo).

کنیا.e: Learned borrowing from Sanskrit कन्या (kanyā). Doublet of کَنِیز (kanīz).
Learned borrowing from Sanskrit कन्या (kanyā). Doublet of کَنِیز (kanīz).

کنیز.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian کنیز (kaniz), from Proto-Iranian *kanyáH (“small girl; maiden”), from Proto-Indo-Iranian *kanyáH. Doublet of کنّیا (kannayā).

کنیسہ.e: Borrowed from Arabic كَنِيسَة (kanīsa). See also Hebrew כְּנֶסֶת (kənesseṯ).

ex.u: میرے بھائی کو پانی دیں۔
ex.e: Please give some water to my elder brother.

ex.u: میں شام کو گھر لوٹوں گا۔
ex.r: ma͠i śām ko ghar lauṭū̃ gā.
ex.e: I will return home in the evening.

کو.e: Inherited from Middle Hindi کوں (kvṉ /⁠kū̃⁠/)., from Old Hindi काहू (kāhū), काहु (kāhu), from Sauraseni Prakrit [Term?], from Sanskrit कक्षे (kákṣe, “in the armpit”), locative singular of कक्ष (kákṣa, “armpit”), from Proto-Indo-Aryan *káṭṣas, from Proto-Indo-Iranian *káćšas, from Proto-Indo-European *kóḱsos (“joint”). Compare Deccani کو (kū), Braj कौ (kau), Assamese -ক (-k). The semantic development of the terms was in the armpit > at the side > towards > to. Deccani (and other Southern dialects of Hindustani) had a parallel development which also includes a locative sense.

کوا.e: Inherited from Middle Hindi کَوا (kau(v)ā /⁠kvā⁠/) (c. 1609), from Sauraseni Prakrit 𑀓𑀸𑀬 (kāya), from Sanskrit काक (kā́ka). Cognates Compare Kashmiri کاو (kāv), Punjabi کاں (kāṉ) and کَن٘و (kaṉv), Sindhi ڪَان (kā̃) and ڪَاءُن (kā̃ũ), Shina کاں (kā̃), Bengali কাউয়া (kauẏa). Doublet of کاگ (kāg).

senses.g: Ivory Coast (a country in West Africa)

ex.u: کوت دیواغ (92 نیوز) مراکش اور تیونس نے آئندہ برس ہونے والے فیفا ورلڈ کپ کیلئے کوالیفائی کر لیا، چار بار کی چیمپئن اٹلی کی قسمت کا فیصلہ کل ہو گا۔
ex.e: Cote D'Ivoire (92 News) Morocco and Tunisia have qualified for the upcoming FIFA World Cup, the fate of four-time champion Italy will be decided tomorrow.

کوتھمیر.e: Inherited from Prakrit 𑀓𑀼𑀢𑁆𑀣𑀼𑀁𑀪𑀭𑀻 (kutthuṃbharī), from Sanskrit कुस्तुम्बरी (kustumbarī).

کوٹ.e: Inherited from Sauraseni Prakrit 𑀓𑁄𑀝𑁆𑀝 (koṭṭa), from Sanskrit कोट्ट (koṭṭa), कोट (koṭa).

senses.g: Kot Addu (a city in Kot Addu district, Punjab, Pakistan)
Kot Abdul Malik (a city in Sheikhupura district, Punjab, Pakistan)

کوٹ عبدالمالک.e: From Urdu کوٹ (koṭ, “fortress”) + Urdu عبدالمالک (“Abdul Malik”).

senses.g: Kotri (a city in Jamshoro district, Sindh, Pakistan)

کوٹری.e: From Sindhi ڪوٽڙي (koṭṛī), Ultimately from Sanskrit कोट (koṭa).

senses.g: name of a fountain or river in Paradise
the name of a chapter in the Qur'an

کوثر.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian کَوثَر (kawsar), from Arabic كَوْثَر (kawṯar). First attested in c. 1611 as Middle Hindi کوثر (kvs̱r /⁠kausar⁠/).

senses.g: a unisex given name from Arabic

کوثر.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian کَوثَر (kawsar), from Arabic كَوْثَر (kawṯar). First attested in c. 1611 as Middle Hindi کوثر (kvs̱r /⁠kausar⁠/).

senses.g: a Kaurava, a descendant of the legendary king Kuru in the Mahabharata
of or belonging to king Kuru, or the Kurus

کوشش.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian کوشِش (kōšiš), from کوشیدن (kušidan, “to try, strive, endeavor”) + ـش (-eš).

کوکا کولا.e: Borrowed from English Coca-Cola. Attested since the 1960s.

ex.u: مَیں نے بَتایا کِہ ناشْتَہ کَر چُکا ہُوں، کوکا کولا پِیُوں گا۔
ex.e: I told you I had breakfast, I'll drink a Coca-Cola.

کوکا کولا.e: Borrowed from English Coca-Cola. Attested since the 1960s.

کوکب.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian کوکب (kawkab), from Arabic كَوْكَب (kawkab).

کولر.e: Borrowed from English cooler or Iranian Persian کولِر (kuler).

senses.g: Columbia (a river in the western United States and Canada)
Columbia (numerous cities, towns, unincorporated communities and census-designated places in the United States)
Columbia (a federal district of the United States)
Cologne (the largest city in North Rhine-Westphalia, in northwestern Germany)

ex.u: کولْہُو کا بَیل
ex.e: (idiomatic) a very hardworking person / a person stuck in a circle or routine

کولہو.e: Inherited from Prakrit *𑀓𑁄𑀮𑁆𑀳𑀼𑀅 (*kŏlhua), from Sanskrit कुठर (kuṭhara). Compare Bengali কলু (kolu).

senses.g: subjective interrogative pronoun used to refer to persons: who

ex.u: کَون بول رَہے ہَیں؟
ex.e: Who is speaking?

کون.e: Inherited from Sauraseni Apabhramsa कवण (kavaṇa), from Sanskrit कः पुनर् (kaḥ punar, “who”). Cognate with Assamese কোন (kün), Gujarati કોણ (koṇ), Marathi कोण (koṇ).

ex.u: کَون سی بَس کَراچی جاتی ہَے؟
ex.r: kaun sī bas karācī jātī hai?
ex.e: Which bus goes to Karachi?

کوہ.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian کوه (kōh). Compare Kashmiri کۄہ (kọh).

کوہ نور.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian کوه نور (kōh-i nūr).

senses.g: Kohat (a city in Kohat district, Khyber Pakhtunkhwa, Pakistan)
